jokes.text: Because you can't spell 'Hilarious' without 'lar,' Larry...Sanders!
jokes.setup: Standard show introduction building anticipation for the host
jokes.punchline: Forced wordplay connecting 'hilarious' to Larry's name
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank - trying too hard to be clever with forced wordplay. Character-appropriate but not particularly witty.

dialogue.line: Because you can't spell 'Hilarious' without 'lar,' Larry...Sanders!

jokes.text: I just bought that line from Wayne Newton. I got that, and I got 'Screw the casino, we're going all night.' And I got, 'ok.'
jokes.setup: Larry acknowledges his weak opening joke
jokes.punchline: Absurd escalation of what else he 'bought' from Wayne Newton, ending with the anticlimactic 'ok'
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-comedy - Larry openly acknowledging his hacky material while making it funnier through self-awareness. The escalation to 'ok' is perfectly deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I just bought that line from Wayne Newton. I got that, and I got 'Screw the casino, we're going all night.' And I got, 'ok.'

jokes.text: Haiti? Uh, where's that? / Well...That is right next to the Dominican Republic. / Oh, right, right. I knew that. That's a great shop, you know.
jokes.setup: Larry asks Hank about Haiti situation, Hank reveals ignorance
jokes.punchline: Hank confuses Haiti the country with a clothing store
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his geographical ignorance leading to consumer confusion. The casual 'That's a great shop' sells the obliviousness completely.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Haiti? Uh, where's that?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well...That is right next to the Dominican Republic.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, right, right. I knew that. That's a great shop, you know.

jokes.text: I bought a safari jacket there once. / I believe you're thinking of the Banana Republic. / Oh! Yeah. That's right.
jokes.setup: Hank's confusion about Haiti continues
jokes.punchline: Even when corrected to Banana Republic, Hank still doesn't realize his mistake about the country
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Hank's confusion, though less punchy than the initial setup.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I bought a safari jacket there once.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I believe you're thinking of the Banana Republic.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh! Yeah. That's right.

jokes.text: You know, if Hank was Secretary of Defense, we'd be invading the Beverly Center right now.
jokes.setup: Hank's geographical confusion established
jokes.punchline: Larry extrapolates Hank's mall-confusion to military consequences
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that escalates the absurdity perfectly. Great economy and a quotable line that feels like classic Larry Sanders.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know, if Hank was Secretary of Defense, we'd be invading the Beverly Center right now.

jokes.text: You didn't think Margaret and I would last a year, did you? Be honest. / Not true. Not true. / I remember what you said. 'Hank, it won't last a year.' / Well, that sounds like me.
jokes.setup: Hank challenges Larry about his marriage pessimism
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate capitulation with 'that sounds like me'
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - caught in his honesty and not even attempting to deny it. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You didn't think Margaret and I would last a year, did you? Be honest.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Not true. Not true.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I remember what you said. 'Hank, it won't last a year.'

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, that sounds like me.

jokes.text: No, nonsense, this is how I've always dreamt of it. Hank, Larry, my wife. Please.
jokes.setup: Larry suggests Hank should celebrate anniversary alone with wife
jokes.punchline: Hank's bizarre romantic fantasy includes Larry as a third wheel
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Hank's codependent relationship with Larry. The earnestness makes it funnier and more disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, nonsense, this is how I've always dreamt of it. Hank, Larry, my wife. Please.

jokes.text: A pekingese, but they don't swim. I think they just sink like a bowling ball, don't they?
jokes.setup: Discussion about dogs that swim
jokes.punchline: Hank's casual observation about small dogs sinking like bowling balls
jokes.explanation: Dark but casual observation that's both absurd and oddly logical. Good visual image.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: A pekingese, but they don't swim. I think they just sink like a bowling ball, don't they?

jokes.text: I do not know how much more of this I can take. / Well, we can talk about current events or sports or-- / No, no, no, I mean Hank.
jokes.setup: Larry thinks Margaret means the dinner conversation is boring
jokes.punchline: Margaret clarifies she means Hank himself is unbearable
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that reveals the episode's central conflict efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: I do not know how much more of this I can take.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, we can talk about current events or sports or--

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: No, no, no, I mean Hank.

jokes.text: Please, let's not show the chimp attacking my balls again. I mean, come on. / But people love that. / No. You're projecting.
jokes.setup: Larry reviewing clips for anniversary show
jokes.punchline: Larry's neurotic assumption that his producer is projecting his own interests
jokes.explanation: Establishes Larry's paranoia and the show's history of physical comedy mishaps.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Please, let's not show the chimp attacking my balls again. I mean, come on.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: But people love that.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No. You're projecting.

jokes.text: The parrot, uh, attacking my balls? / Yes. Pass.
jokes.setup: Continuation of animal-attack clips discussion
jokes.punchline: Larry's weary recognition that even the parrot attacked his genitals
jokes.explanation: Builds the running gag but is getting repetitive. Larry's resignation sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The parrot, uh, attacking my balls?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Yes. Pass.

jokes.text: The pie eating scene, it has no precursor in talk show history.
jokes.setup: Phil's grandiose claims about Larry's clips
jokes.punchline: Treating a pie-eating scene as historically significant
jokes.explanation: Good satire of TV producer hyperbole, though not particularly sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: The pie eating scene, it has no precursor in talk show history.

jokes.text: You never know what might happen, but it'll probably involve my nuts.
jokes.setup: Larry's promotional segment for anniversary show
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of the show's running gag
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor that acknowledges the absurdity while playing into it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You never know what might happen, but it'll probably involve my nuts.

jokes.text: You guys have a lot of yuk-em-ups in there, huh? You have some good laughs?
jokes.setup: Hank discovers Larry and producer laughing at his wedding clip
jokes.punchline: Hank's forced casual tone while clearly hurt
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's passive-aggressive hurt feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You guys have a lot of yuk-em-ups in there, huh? You have some good laughs?

jokes.text: My marriage is one big joke to you, isn't it? / That is not the case. / Come here, Hank. Come on, sweetheart. Listen, I know you're going through a tough time now, you know? Believe me, I've been there. / Well, I'm not there. I don't know where there is.
jokes.setup: Artie tries to console Hank about his marital problems
jokes.punchline: Hank's literal interpretation of 'being there'
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay that shows Hank's obtuseness in emotional moments.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: My marriage is one big joke to you, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: That is not the case.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Come here, Hank. Come on, sweetheart. Listen, I know you're going through a tough time now, you know? Believe me, I've been there.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not there. I don't know where there is.

jokes.text: It's not a therapist, darling. It's my divorce lawyer. He's done a lot of bulk work for me.
jokes.setup: Hank resists therapy suggestion
jokes.punchline: Artie's casual reference to his divorce lawyer doing 'bulk work'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie line - treating divorce lawyers like a bulk commodity reveals his character history efficiently and darkly.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: It's not a therapist, darling. It's my divorce lawyer. He's done a lot of bulk work for me.

jokes.text: A man's sexual peak is at 18. You know, scientists have proven this, but, hey, I think I hang in there pretty good for a 38-year-old guy, you know. / Hank, that is not the problem. / I know it isn't! And, besides, you are 48.
jokes.setup: Hank explains their marital problems as age-related performance issues
jokes.punchline: Margaret corrects his age, revealing he's even older than his already-old example
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that shows both Hank's denial and Margaret's exasperation.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: A man's sexual peak is at 18. You know, scientists have proven this, but, hey, I think I hang in there pretty good for a 38-year-old guy, you know.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: Hank, that is not the problem.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I know it isn't! And, besides, you are 48.

jokes.text: What--what's a 2-minute warning? / A 2-minute warning is when either party just separates for 2 minutes before either one says or does something that they're just gonna regret later, ok? 2 minutes. / Hmm, and which one of your bimbo ex-girlfriends taught you that?
jokes.setup: Larry explains a relationship cooling-off technique
jokes.punchline: Margaret immediately violates the spirit of the technique with an insult
jokes.explanation: Smart ironic structure - the advice is immediately undercut by the person who should benefit from it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What--what's a 2-minute warning?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A 2-minute warning is when either party just separates for 2 minutes before either one says or does something that they're just gonna regret later, ok? 2 minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: Hmm, and which one of your bimbo ex-girlfriends taught you that?

jokes.text: Are you fucking my wife? / What? / Just answer the question. You--you're fucking Margaret, aren't you? / No, I'm fucking Larry.
jokes.setup: Hank's paranoid accusation about Larry and Margaret
jokes.punchline: Larry's deadpan response claiming to be sleeping with Larry instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. The deadpan delivery would make this a standout moment - unexpected and perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Are you fucking my wife?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Just answer the question. You--you're fucking Margaret, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, I'm fucking Larry.

jokes.text: See, at first I just chalked it up to female problems. / Mm-hmm. She having trouble with her-- The, uh-- / Oh, no. No, not that. I mean, on the other hand, how would I know? I haven't been down there in months. She could have grown a dick over the summer. I wouldn't have a clue.
jokes.setup: Hank discussing his wife's problems and their lack of intimacy
jokes.punchline: Absurd escalation about not knowing if she's grown male anatomy
jokes.explanation: Great escalation that's both absurd and sadly revealing about their relationship. The visual image is memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: See, at first I just chalked it up to female problems.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm. She having trouble with her-- The, uh--

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, no. No, not that. I mean, on the other hand, how would I know? I haven't been down there in months. She could have grown a dick over the summer. I wouldn't have a clue.

jokes.text: Yes, yes, we're in show business, but I know in my heart of hearts you would never fuck my wife. / Not without your go-ahead. / Exactly.
jokes.setup: Hank expressing trust in Larry's character
jokes.punchline: Larry's response implies he'd need permission rather than having moral restraint
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that subverts expectations completely. The fact that Hank agrees makes it even funnier - shows their twisted dynamic perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, we're in show business, but I know in my heart of hearts you would never fuck my wife.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Not without your go-ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Your entire life revolves around Larry. Your mood depends on what Larry said or what Larry did. / I think this is that crazy therapist. / No. If Larry was mean to you, then you're miserable, but if Larry was nice, then you're the happiest man on earth.
jokes.setup: Margaret's analysis of Hank's Larry obsession
jokes.punchline: Hank's deflection blaming a therapist for putting ideas in her head
jokes.explanation: Good character insight that advances the plot, though not particularly funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: Your entire life revolves around Larry. Your mood depends on what Larry said or what Larry did.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I think this is that crazy therapist.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: No. If Larry was mean to you, then you're miserable, but if Larry was nice, then you're the happiest man on earth.

jokes.text: Remember when you forgot my birthday? Have you ever forgotten Larry's birthday? / Well, now, there's a reason for that, because Larry's birthday is just easy to remember. It's December 19th. It just rolls right out, you know, whereas, uh, your birthday is--
jokes.setup: Margaret points out Hank's priorities regarding birthdays
jokes.punchline: Hank proving her point by knowing Larry's birthday but trailing off about hers
jokes.explanation: Perfect demonstration of Margaret's point - Hank immediately proves exactly what she's talking about without realizing it.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: Remember when you forgot my birthday? Have you ever forgotten Larry's birthday?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, now, there's a reason for that, because Larry's birthday is just easy to remember. It's December 19th. It just rolls right out, you know, whereas, uh, your birthday is--

jokes.text: I got it! I got it! I'm coming!
jokes.setup: Larry calling for his script binder during marital crisis
jokes.punchline: Hank immediately abandons wife to serve Larry
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical demonstration of Margaret's point. Hank literally runs away from marriage crisis to serve Larry.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I got it! I got it! I'm coming!

jokes.text: You should have married Larry. / Aw, don't do this. / Good-bye. Don't do this.
jokes.setup: Margaret's final statement before leaving
jokes.punchline: The brutal truth about Hank's real emotional relationship
jokes.explanation: Devastating line that crystallizes the entire episode's theme. Dark but perfectly earned through all the evidence we've seen.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: You should have married Larry.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Aw, don't do this.

dialogue.speaker: Margaret
dialogue.line: Good-bye.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Don't do this.

jokes.text: If you're looking for a place to live when she gets the house, it's no problem, buddy. Here's a brochure for the Oakwood Apartments. It's fully furnished, dishes in the kitchen, 2 pools, whole 9 yards. And the complex is just loaded with fellow divorce victims. So whenever you get the urge to piss all over your ex, everybody joins in. You'll have a ball.
jokes.setup: Artie offering practical divorce advice
jokes.punchline: Describing a divorce complex as a community of bitter ex-husbands
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy that shows Artie's pragmatic cynicism. The 'you'll have a ball' is nicely ironic.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If you're looking for a place to live when she gets the house, it's no problem, buddy. Here's a brochure for the Oakwood Apartments. It's fully furnished, dishes in the kitchen, 2 pools, whole 9 yards. And the complex is just loaded with fellow divorce victims. So whenever you get the urge to piss all over your ex, everybody joins in. You'll have a ball.

jokes.text: Well, uh, how about your wife? / Oh, well, you know, we had a good year, but that's over.
jokes.setup: Larry's attempt to redirect Hank toward his actual relationship
jokes.punchline: Hank's casual dismissal of his marriage on live TV
jokes.explanation: Brutally casual dismissal that's both shocking and perfectly in character. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, uh, how about your wife?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, well, you know, we had a good year, but that's over.

jokes.text: Hank seems in good spirits. / Who's he dancing with? / Himself. / Oh.
jokes.setup: Observing Hank at the wrap party
jokes.punchline: Revelation that Hank is dancing alone
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag that reinforces Hank's isolation theme. Economical but not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hank seems in good spirits.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Who's he dancing with?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Himself.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: But you'll live through it like I did. And you're-- you're pissed...angry...but alive, god damn it, alive. Whoo!
jokes.setup: Artie giving drunk divorce advice
jokes.punchline: His increasingly manic celebration of survival
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for drunk Artie, though the humor is more situational than sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: But you'll live through it like I did. And you're-- you're pissed...angry...but alive, god damn it, alive. Whoo!

jokes.text: You're not an original member of the O'Jays, are you, by any chance?
jokes.setup: Artie's drunken state leading to random thoughts
jokes.punchline: Completely random question about 70s R&B group membership
jokes.explanation: Random absurdist humor that fits drunk Artie, though not particularly meaningful.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You're not an original member of the O'Jays, are you, by any chance?

jokes.text: Well, you know, Larry, there's a deep place inside us all, a very deep place. The trick is you gotta know how to get there. And that place is...your desk, top right-hand drawer, yellow pills. Take a few of those, a shot of bourbon...you'll go immediately to this place.
jokes.setup: Hank building up philosophical advice about finding inner peace
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's talking about drug-assisted numbness, not enlightenment
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that subverts pseudo-philosophical advice with chemical reality. Dark ending that fits the show's tone.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Larry, there's a deep place inside us all, a very deep place. The trick is you gotta know how to get there. And that place is...your desk, top right-hand drawer, yellow pills. Take a few of those, a shot of bourbon...you'll go immediately to this place.
